title: Before They Became: A Tale of Banjhakri and Lamlamey
author: DewDrop Stories
description: A mystical journey into the heart of Himalayan folklore, where the forest spirit Banjhakri and the golden-voiced Lamlamey were not always what they became. Before the legends, before the fear and the reverence — there was a beginning.

chapters.title: Chapter 1: Of Light and Love
chapters.content:
Come here. Come close.

Let me tell you a story you think you’ve already heard - the tale of Bhanjhakri and Lamlamey.

But stories are not always what they seem.

Before they were names spoken in hushed voices, before they became something the forest kept, they were only a boy and a girl.

A story of friendship.
Of love.

You may have heard it before, passed down in whispers from your grandparents, shaped and reshaped with every telling.

But this one, this one is my grandmother’s. The story she grew up listening to in the dense forest villages of Dingla.

So come here.
Come close.

In the late 1700s, by the Arun Valley, there was a settlement that clung to the curve of the river as if it had always belonged there.

Houses of stone and mud rested along the hillside, their roofs darkened by rain and time. Thin trails wound between them - narrow paths shaped not by planning, but by footsteps repeated over generations.

Below, the Arun moved steadily, never in a hurry, its voice so constant that the villagers no longer heard it - until it was gone.

In the mornings, mist rose from the water and settled over the fields, softening the world before the sun burned it away.

By afternoon, the hills stood clear and green, and on certain days, far beyond them, the white peaks of Mount Everest and Makalu appeared. Distant, unmoving, like something that belonged to another world.

The forest began where the fields ended. Not suddenly, but enough that people knew where not to go.

And when night fell, it fell completely.

No lamps reached beyond the last house.
No voices carried past the edge of the village.

Only the river remained and whatever listened from the dark.

Most of the people in the village were Kirati, deeply rooted in their belief in the spirits of the forest. Others lived among them too—families who farmed the land and followed different ways of life.

Among them were the Chhetris. They lived side by side, in a kind of quiet harmony but love did not cross those boundaries.

Kingdoms were shifting. A nation was slowly taking shape.

In that village was a boy born into a family of shamans, though his heart was drawn elsewhere.

He was named Kiran, for he was born on the first day of spring, after a long and bitter winter.

And in the quiet of night, the boy would whisper to himself before sleep - Kiran Bahadur Rai… a soldier.

He had seen them - men crossing the valley with purpose, with steel, with something larger than the village in their eyes.

He wanted that.

And yet, when spring returned and the hills turned green again, Kiran could always be found by the river, playing his murali.

As if the forest listened, and the wind carried it further than he ever could.

A few houses away stood a different kind of household, one known for its sons.

Warriors, the villagers said. Men who would one day give their lives for their kingdom.

And yet, in that house, after five sons, a girl was born.

Her mother, with her last breath, named her Maya.

Maya Chhetri.

Her brothers guarded her closely, never letting her wander near the edge of the forest.

She did not play like the other children. She did not run as far.

And so, quietly, she began to dream.

Not of duty, but of freedom.

Maya, who was rarely allowed beyond the courtyard, learned to notice the smallest things. The way wildflowers grew between stones, how their colours changed with the light.

By spring and summer, her hands were never empty.

She wove what she could find into crowns and small garlands, as if making something of the world she was not allowed to wander.

Girls were married young in those days. Sometimes as young as five.

It was only a ritual but even rituals had weight.

Maya was eight, and the threads around her wrist had been tied before she was old enough to ask why.

She knew, in the quiet way children know things no one explains, that one day she would leave -

her home,
her brothers,
her hills.

And she would leave him too.

The boy by the river.

The one who asked too many questions.

Who made her crowns of wildflowers.
Who brought her chestnuts, berries, and wild pears.

Who led her, sometimes, just close enough to the forest edge where the kaphal trees grew. Never further.

When she grew quiet, he would play the murali to make her smile again.

And so they were,

two children from different worlds.

One who dreamed of flying free,
and one who dreamed of dying for something greater than himself.

And yet,

they were still children.

They chased butterflies through the tall grass, never quite catching them, never quite wanting to.

They ran where they were told not to, stopping just before the forest edge. Laughing as if they had crossed something they hadn’t.

Kiran would lie on his back by the river, staring at the sky as if it held answers he could not yet ask.

Maya would sit beside him, weaving flowers into shapes that had no purpose except that her hands needed something to hold.

Sometimes, they said nothing at all.

And sometimes, they spoke of things that would never come true -

of distant lands,
of lives beyond the hills,
of futures that felt close enough to believe in.

When the wind carried Kiran’s music further than it should have, she would close her eyes -

as if, for a moment, she had already become the freedom she dreamed of.

And when she laughed -

not softly,
not carefully,
but fully, without thought -

Kiran would forget, just for a while, that he wanted to be anything else.

The world had not yet asked anything of them.

Not truly.

And in that small, passing time, they belonged only to themselves -

and to each other.

chapters.title: Chapter 2: Of Spring and Unspoken Things
chapters.content:
By the time the hills turned green again, they were no longer children. Not in the way the village remembered them.

It was spring again but it did not arrive gently.

There was something restless in the air that year, something that settled in the spaces between words. The forest felt closer. Not in distance, but in the way its silence carried further than before.

Even the elders spoke of it differently.

And when they did, they spoke of him - the one who resided beyond the place where even the brave turned back. The villagers believed there was something in the forest.

A presence. Jungli dewta, they called it.

No one could agree on when it had first appeared, or whether it had ever truly been seen. But those who went to forage, hunt, or gather herbs spoke of glimpses -
of a figure where there should have been none,
of whispers that did not belong to the wind.

They said it watched over the forest and all that lived within it. And so, each year, the Kirati people gathered at the edge of the trees to offer prayers - asking for good harvests, for protection, and for the river to remain kind.

For when it did not, it took without warning.
Houses, cattle, sometimes more.

And still, no great disaster had come in many years. The elders said that meant the forest was listening. Not everyone feared the forest in the same way.

Kiran’s voice had deepened, though he still carried his murali to the river. Maya no longer ran barefoot through the courtyard, though her hands still reached for flowers without thinking.
They had grown into the kind of silence that did not need explaining.

And yet, that spring, they found themselves drawn closer to the edge of the forest than they had ever been before. The new year was close.

It showed first in small ways…
in the way the air softened,
in the way the older women began preparing earlier than usual,
in the way the girls of the village gathered together, laughing over things Maya was only just beginning to understand.

They stained their hands with colour.
Deep reds and soft browns pressed into their palms, patterns that lingered long after the days had passed.

Maya had watched them the year before. This year, she did not want to only watch.

“It grows near the forest,” one of her sisters-in-law had said, almost carelessly.

“The flower that stains. If you find it fresh.”

That was all it took.

Kiran did not ask why when she told him. He only nodded, as if it had already been decided.

They went in the late afternoon, when the village was busiest - when no one would notice where they had gone. The path they took was not new. Only longer.

The fields gave way slowly, as they always did -
the last of the open land,
the last place where voices still carried.

And then the forest.

They did not stop where they usually did. Not that day.

“It should be further in,” Maya said, though she did not know how she knew.

Kiran followed anyway.

It was Maya who found the flower. Small, hidden beneath the shade of taller leaves, its petals deep and red - the kind that stained the skin if pressed long enough. She knelt, her face lighting in a way Kiran had not seen before.

“This one,” she whispered.

They had already gone further than before. Not by much but enough that the sounds of the village had disappeared completely. The air felt different there.

Still. As if even the wind had chosen not to pass through.

Maya reached for the flower.
And paused.

Not because she saw him but because something in the forest had gone quiet in a way that did not feel natural.

Kiran felt it too.

A stillness that settled not around them, but on them.

He stood where the trees thinned slightly, just beyond where the light could reach clearly.
Not hidden.
Not revealed.

As if he had always been there, and they had only just learned how to see him.

Neither of them spoke.

The stories returned all at once -
whispered warnings,
half-believed truths.

Jungli dewta.

He did not move toward them. He did not reach.

He only stood watching…
not with anger,
not with kindness,
but with something older than both.

Maya did not feel afraid. Not in the way she thought she would. But she did not feel safe either.

Kiran took a step forward before he understood why. The sound of his foot against the earth seemed louder than it should have been.

The figure shifted.
Not away.
Not closer.

Just enough that the light changed and for a moment, it felt as if the forest itself had breathed.

Maya’s hand closed around Kiran’s wrist.
Not pulling. Not yet.

Just holding, as if to make sure he did not take another step. And then, as quietly as he had appeared, he was no longer there.

The forest returned all at once.
The sound of leaves.
The distant call of something unseen.
The world, as it had been.

Only the flower remained in Maya’s hand - crushed slightly, its colour already beginning to stain her skin.

It was the first time she had held his hand. Not in play. Not by accident.

And though she let go once the forest released them, something of it remained - as if the space between them had been altered in a way neither of them knew how to undo.

The path was the same. The fields were the same. The village, unchanged.

And yet, nothing felt as it had before.

That spring, they were no longer children. Not in the way they had been.

And still, in the small spaces that belonged only to them, they held on -
to the river,
to the flowers,
to each other.

As if, despite everything that was coming, they could remain what they had always been.

Just a boy and a girl,
and the quiet promise of years they still believed were theirs.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Of Summer and What Was Lost
chapters.content:
By the time summer returned, a year had already taken more than it had given.

The hills were greener than before.
The days longer.
The river louder in its passing.

And yet, nothing felt as it once had.

They were older now. Fourteen, and fifteen.
And standing, without knowing it, at the edge of their last summer together.

They tried to meet as often as they could. As if time had begun to move faster, and they had only just noticed.

“Tomorrow,” Kiran had said the evening before.

“I’ll come early.”

Maya nodded, though she knew better than to hold too tightly to promises. Still, she arrived before the sun had climbed fully into the sky.

The place was the same - the clearing just before the forest deepened, the path they knew without thinking.

She waited where the light fell softest. At first, she did not mind. He had been late before. She gathered flowers while she waited. Small ones, nothing rare, weaving them into something without deciding what it would be.

The sun climbed higher. The shade shifted. The sounds of the day grew louder, then quieter again.
Each time the leaves stirred, she looked up, expecting footsteps she knew by heart.

He did not come.

By afternoon, she had stopped weaving. The flowers lay loose in her lap, their stems drying slowly in the heat. And when the light began to soften, she stood - not all at once, but in the way one does when waiting no longer has a reason. She left without looking back.

Kiran did not come that day.

He had left before sunrise, called by his father to another village across the hills. There were things he needed to learn, they had said. Things that could not wait. And so he walked beside his father, carrying what he was told, listening to what he did not yet understand while somewhere else, time passed without him.

He walked faster on the journey home, as if speed alone could return him to the moment he had already missed. But by the time they reached the village, it was dark.

The next day, Kiran came earlier than he ever had before.

The clearing was empty. He waited anyway. The morning stretched slowly into afternoon.

The same light. The same wind.

But without her, everything felt unfinished.

He played the murali once, softly, as if the sound might reach her where he could not. It faded too quickly.

Maya did not come that day.

Not because she chose not to but because, for the first time, she could not. There was more to do now. More hands needed in the house. More eyes watching. Her time no longer moved the way it used to. And though she thought of the clearing - of the place where he would be waiting, she did not know how to leave.

And somewhere between those two days, without either of them knowing, something had already begun to slip out of reach.

After that, they both tried. More than before.

Kiran came earlier the next day and found only the quiet of the clearing - the place where she would have stood.
Maya came later that same afternoon and found only the path he would have taken to leave.

The days that followed were no different.
He came when he could.
She came when she was allowed.

Sometimes he waited. Sometimes she did.
But never at the same time.

The clearing remained as it always had been.
Only now, it held their absence more than it held them.

It was nearly a week later when they saw each other again. Not where they had planned but where life had taken them instead.

Maya had come to the river with her sisters-in-law, carrying clothes, carrying water, carrying more than she used to.

The river moved steadily beside them, as if nothing in the world had changed.

Kiran came with the others - boys from the village laughing too loudly, throwing themselves into the water to escape the summer heat.

He had not expected to see her. And yet, he did.

It was not sudden. Not the way he had imagined their meeting would be. Just a glance, and then another. Held longer than it should have been.

Maya lowered her gaze first. But not before he had seen it, the quiet relief she had not meant to show. It took time before they stood close enough to speak. Not alone, never alone now but near enough.

“You didn’t come,” he said, softer than he meant to.

“I did,” she replied. And after a moment… “You weren’t there.”

He looked away first this time. Not in avoidance, but in the quiet understanding of something that had no answer.

“I had to go with my father,” he said. “To another village.”

The words felt smaller than the reason behind them.
Maya nodded. She did not ask more.

“From now on…” he began, then stopped, as if choosing words that could hold more than they should.

“From now on, I’ll come,” he said finally. “You won’t have to wait.”

She looked at him then - not with doubt, not with certainty, but with something in between.

“Then come here,” she said quietly. “By the river. I can always find a reason to come here.”

It was not a plan. Not really. Just something they could hold on to for as long as it would last.
The voices around them grew louder. The river carried everything away as it always did.

And still, for a moment longer, they stood there - as if time had paused just enough to let them believe it had not already begun to move without them.

And for the first time in many days, neither of them had to imagine the other being there.

Kiran came first. He had learned to. To arrive before the light grew too strong, before the river filled with voices that did not belong to them. He sat by the bank, the murali resting loosely in his hands. Not playing - just holding it, as if the habit alone was enough.

The river moved as it always had.
Unhurried.
Unconcerned.
It did not know what was changing.

He heard her before he saw her. Not her voice but the quiet rhythm of footsteps he had learned without trying.

Maya came down the path slowly, carrying less than before, but somehow more. She did not call out to him. She never did.

“You came early,” she said when she reached him.

“So you wouldn’t have to wait.”

She smiled not fully, but enough.

They sat beside each other, close enough that the space between them no longer felt like distance. For a while, they said nothing. The river spoke for them.

“Do you still go with your father?” she asked after some time.

Kiran nodded.

“More than before.”

He turned the murali slowly in his hands.

“He says I need to learn everything now.”

“Do you want to?”

He did not answer right away. A small stone shifted beneath his foot as he pressed it into the earth.

“No,” he said finally.

Not with anger.
Not with defiance.
Just truth.

“But I will.”

Maya watched the water instead of him.

“You always said you wanted to be a soldier.”

“I did.”

He smiled slightly, though it did not stay long.

“I don’t know if I still can.”

The words settled between them. Not heavy but final in a way neither of them tried to change.

“Things change,” Maya said quietly.

He looked at her then.

“Do they have to?”

She did not answer. Instead, she reached into the fold of her shawl and took out a few flowers. Smaller than the ones she used to bring. Fewer.

“I didn’t have time to make anything,” she said.

“It’s alright,” he replied.

And for a moment - it almost was.

He lifted the murali then, playing something soft - not a tune she knew,
not one he had played before.

Something unfinished.

When the sound faded, the quiet that followed felt different. As if it had been waiting for something.

“Kiran…” She said his name the way one does when the next words cannot be undone.

He lowered the murali.

“In a month,” she said, “they’re coming.”

The river did not stop. The wind did not change. But something in him did.

“My in-laws,” she added, though she did not need to. “They’ll take me.”

He did not speak. Not because he had nothing to say but because nothing he could say would stay. The space between them remained the same.

And yet, it had never felt so far.

With a month left, time no longer moved as it once had. It shortened. Folded in on itself.
Their meetings grew smaller. No longer hours, but moments.
A few minutes by the river every other day. Enough to see each other. Not enough to stay.

The forest faded from their thoughts. Not forgotten, just replaced by things that could no longer be ignored.

Maya’s days filled with lessons she could not refuse.
The kitchen.
The fire.

The careful preparation of things she would soon be expected to know without being taught. Her hands, once stained with flowers, now carried the scent of spices and smoke.

Kiran’s days grew heavier. He no longer followed his father, he worked beside him. Going from house to house, village to village, where illness lingered without name and people waited for things they could not see.

And then - his father fell ill.

It came quietly. As such things often did.

“Why?” Kiran had asked. “Why can’t it be cured?”

He had seen it done. He had watched his father bring relief where there had been none. His father did not answer immediately.

Only after a long silence - “When they want something back,” he said, “they take it.”

After that, Kiran did not go to the river. But still, he left something behind. A few flowers placed quietly near the place they always sat.
Not arranged.
Not perfect.
Just enough.

And Maya, when she came, knew. That he had been there. That he had not forgotten.

The day before they were to come for her, he returned. Not as he had before but as someone who had already begun to carry what he did not yet understand.

They stood by the river, closer than they had ever been, and yet with something between them neither could cross.

“In another life,” Maya said, her voice softer than it had ever been, “may we be born the same.”

“In the same place. In the same world. In the same community.”

She looked at him then not as she had before.

“So we can be friends again.”

A pause. “And maybe… more than that.”

Kiran did not smile. But something in his eyes held onto the words as if they might stay.

“I’ll come,” he said. “When they take you.”

“I’ll be there.”

She nodded. “I’ll look for you.”

The next day, the house was filled.
Voices.
Movement.
The quiet of before replaced by something final.

Maya was dressed in red - wrapped, adorned, made into something that did not belong to the girl she had once been. Gold rested on her where there had once been nothing. She looked older than she felt.

When she stepped into the palanquin, she cried -
for her home,
for her family,
for the hills that had known her before she knew herself.

And still, she looked for him. For the one who had been there before all of this.
Her light.

He was not there.
For a moment, she wondered if he had forgotten. But only for a moment. She knew him better than that. Something had happened.

It had.

His father had worsened that same day. And though for a brief morning it seemed he might recover, by evening, he fell. With what breath remained, he placed the weight of everything into Kiran’s hands.

His mother.
His siblings.
His place.

Kiran did not leave his side.
Not when the water was needed.
Not when the silence grew longer.

And when it ended, it ended quietly.

By the time Kiran ran, the valley had already begun to swallow the road.

The procession had moved far beyond where he could follow. All that remained was distance.

A small line of figures.
A fading path.
And a single palanquin carrying someone he would not reach again.

And though the distance between them grew beyond what either could cross, neither of them let go.

Not of the river.
Not of the quiet they had shared.
Not of each other.

For some things, once held, do not leave - even when everything else does.
